channel.title: Best Mortgages For You – UK Property & Mortgage News
channel.description: Latest UK property and mortgage news from leading industry blogs.

item.title: Where Can You Order MDF Cut to Size Online? A Buyer’s Guide from Harlow Bros
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

For homeowners and property developers undertaking renovation projects, sourcing quality MDF (medium-density fibreboard) cut to precise specifications can streamline DIY work and professional builds alike. Several reputable online timber merchants now offer this service, though selecting the right supplier requires careful consideration beyond price alone.

Key Considerations When Ordering MDF Online

When purchasing pre-cut MDF sheets online, several factors could influence the success of a project:

  • Material expertise – Suppliers with knowledgeable staff can advise on appropriate thicknesses and grades for specific applications
  • Cutting precision – Accurate measurements and clean cuts prevent material wastage and fitting issues
  • Delivery options – Reliable logistics ensure materials arrive undamaged and on schedule
  • Product range – Multiple board options allow for selection of moisture-resistant or fire-rated variants where needed

Finding Reputable Online Timber Merchants

Homeowners might consider these approaches when researching MDF suppliers:

  • Check independent review platforms for customer feedback on cutting accuracy and delivery reliability
  • Compare sample pricing for identical specifications across multiple suppliers
  • Verify whether merchants offer technical support for product selection
  • Assess delivery costs and timeframes, particularly for urgent projects

While cost remains important, the most suitable supplier typically combines competitive pricing with expert guidance and dependable service. This balanced approach can help avoid costly mistakes and project delays when working with pre-cut MDF materials.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

For homeowners and property developers undertaking renovation projects, sourcing quality MDF (medium-density fibreboard) cut to precise specifications can streamline DIY work and professional builds alike. Several reputable online timber merchants now offer this service, though selecting the right supplier requires careful consideration beyond price alone.

Key Considerations When Ordering MDF Online

When purchasing pre-cut MDF sheets online, several factors could influence the success of a project:

  • Material expertise – Suppliers with knowledgeable staff can advise on appropriate thicknesses and grades for specific applications
  • Cutting precision – Accurate measurements and clean cuts prevent material wastage and fitting issues
  • Delivery options – Reliable logistics ensure materials arrive undamaged and on schedule
  • Product range – Multiple board options allow for selection of moisture-resistant or fire-rated variants where needed

Finding Reputable Online Timber Merchants

Homeowners might consider these approaches when researching MDF suppliers:

  • Check independent review platforms for customer feedback on cutting accuracy and delivery reliability
  • Compare sample pricing for identical specifications across multiple suppliers
  • Verify whether merchants offer technical support for product selection
  • Assess delivery costs and timeframes, particularly for urgent projects

While cost remains important, the most suitable supplier typically combines competitive pricing with expert guidance and dependable service. This balanced approach can help avoid costly mistakes and project delays when working with pre-cut MDF materials.

item.title: The £3bn shake-up few agents and landlords saw coming
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Proposed Deposit Protection Changes Could Impact £3bn in Tenant Funds

Government proposals to reform tenancy deposit protection in England and Wales may affect over £3bn held in insured schemes, according to analysis by The Letting Partnership. The plans could require all deposits to be held in custodial arrangements, replacing the current system where landlords and letting agents may opt for insured protection models.

Current figures suggest approximately 4.7 million deposits are protected across both nations, with 45.6% (2.1 million) using insured schemes. While custodial schemes account for a slight majority (54.4%) of protected deposits, insured arrangements represent the larger share of monetary value – holding £3bn compared to £2.5bn in custodial schemes.

Industry Debates Potential Transition Challenges

The proposals have sparked discussion across the lettings sector regarding the future of deposit protection. Key considerations include:

  • The operational impact on landlords and agents currently using insured schemes
  • How existing insured deposits might transition to custodial protection
  • Whether a phased approach would see new tenancies using custodial schemes while existing arrangements run their course

Chris Mason, COO of The Letting Partnership, noted: "The debate often focuses on who should hold deposits, but the fundamental issue concerns transparency. Neither schemes, underwriters nor government currently have full visibility of the cash position behind these liabilities at any given time."

Mason added that while the government appears to be addressing genuine concerns about tenant money transparency, the industry could face a complex transition lasting several years if changes are implemented. This might require agents to operate both protection models simultaneously during the switchover period.

As consultations continue, stakeholders across the property sector will be monitoring developments closely, particularly regarding how any reforms might balance consumer protection with practical implementation challenges.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Proposed Deposit Protection Changes Could Impact £3bn in Tenant Funds

Government proposals to reform tenancy deposit protection in England and Wales may affect over £3bn held in insured schemes, according to analysis by The Letting Partnership. The plans could require all deposits to be held in custodial arrangements, replacing the current system where landlords and letting agents may opt for insured protection models.

Current figures suggest approximately 4.7 million deposits are protected across both nations, with 45.6% (2.1 million) using insured schemes. While custodial schemes account for a slight majority (54.4%) of protected deposits, insured arrangements represent the larger share of monetary value – holding £3bn compared to £2.5bn in custodial schemes.

Industry Debates Potential Transition Challenges

The proposals have sparked discussion across the lettings sector regarding the future of deposit protection. Key considerations include:

  • The operational impact on landlords and agents currently using insured schemes
  • How existing insured deposits might transition to custodial protection
  • Whether a phased approach would see new tenancies using custodial schemes while existing arrangements run their course

Chris Mason, COO of The Letting Partnership, noted: "The debate often focuses on who should hold deposits, but the fundamental issue concerns transparency. Neither schemes, underwriters nor government currently have full visibility of the cash position behind these liabilities at any given time."

Mason added that while the government appears to be addressing genuine concerns about tenant money transparency, the industry could face a complex transition lasting several years if changes are implemented. This might require agents to operate both protection models simultaneously during the switchover period.

As consultations continue, stakeholders across the property sector will be monitoring developments closely, particularly regarding how any reforms might balance consumer protection with practical implementation challenges.

item.title: Vast majority of tenants still can’t find what they’re looking for
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Rental Market Challenges Persist Despite Tenant Protections

New research highlights a growing divide between tenant rights and rental market realities. Whilst 89% of landlords acknowledge that recent legislation, including the Renters’ Rights Act, has strengthened tenants’ position, many renters still struggle to find suitable accommodation. The LRG Spring 2026 Lettings Report reveals that 78% of tenants searching for a property in the past two years faced fewer options than expected, with affordability and limited availability being key barriers.

The figures paint a challenging picture for renters. A quarter of tenants wished to move in the last year but were unable to, with reasons ranging from financial constraints to a lack of suitable properties. Only 5% successfully relocated during this period. Rising rents add further pressure, with the ONS reporting a 3.5% annual increase, pushing the average monthly private rent to £1,381 in April 2026.

Supply Shortages and Homeownership Hurdles

The rental market’s supply crisis shows little sign of easing. Data from Rightmove indicates available rental stock remains 33% lower than a decade ago, whilst Zoopla reports levels still 20-30% below pre-pandemic figures across all regions. For those considering homeownership as an alternative, the outlook remains difficult. A third of tenants admitted they would still be unable to buy even if house prices fell significantly in their area, with just 16% stating they would act immediately.

Allison Thompson, chief lettings officer at Leaders (part of LRG), noted: "Landlords and tenants are describing the same market from different perspectives. Landlords feel the impact of legislative changes, whilst tenants face the daily reality of limited choice and rising costs."

Whilst regulatory reforms have strengthened tenant protections, the fundamental issue of supply and affordability continues to dominate the rental market. For many, the search for a suitable home remains an uphill battle.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Rental Market Challenges Persist Despite Tenant Protections

New research highlights a growing divide between tenant rights and rental market realities. Whilst 89% of landlords acknowledge that recent legislation, including the Renters’ Rights Act, has strengthened tenants’ position, many renters still struggle to find suitable accommodation. The LRG Spring 2026 Lettings Report reveals that 78% of tenants searching for a property in the past two years faced fewer options than expected, with affordability and limited availability being key barriers.

The figures paint a challenging picture for renters. A quarter of tenants wished to move in the last year but were unable to, with reasons ranging from financial constraints to a lack of suitable properties. Only 5% successfully relocated during this period. Rising rents add further pressure, with the ONS reporting a 3.5% annual increase, pushing the average monthly private rent to £1,381 in April 2026.

Supply Shortages and Homeownership Hurdles

The rental market’s supply crisis shows little sign of easing. Data from Rightmove indicates available rental stock remains 33% lower than a decade ago, whilst Zoopla reports levels still 20-30% below pre-pandemic figures across all regions. For those considering homeownership as an alternative, the outlook remains difficult. A third of tenants admitted they would still be unable to buy even if house prices fell significantly in their area, with just 16% stating they would act immediately.

Allison Thompson, chief lettings officer at Leaders (part of LRG), noted: "Landlords and tenants are describing the same market from different perspectives. Landlords feel the impact of legislative changes, whilst tenants face the daily reality of limited choice and rising costs."

Whilst regulatory reforms have strengthened tenant protections, the fundamental issue of supply and affordability continues to dominate the rental market. For many, the search for a suitable home remains an uphill battle.

item.title: Setting rents just got more complicated – are you up to speed with the changes?
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The Renters’ Rights Act has introduced significant changes to how rents are set and managed in the private rented sector, leaving letting agents with new responsibilities and challenges. With the legislation now in effect, agents are required to adapt their processes to ensure compliance, particularly when it comes to evidencing market rents and handling tenant requests. To help navigate these changes, industry experts are offering guidance, including a recent webinar hosted by Rightmove.

Understanding the New Rent-Setting Requirements

One of the key aspects of the Renters’ Rights Act is the requirement for letting agents to provide clear evidence when setting or reviewing rents. This means agents must now document their decisions thoroughly, ensuring they can justify rental amounts based on market conditions. The legislation aims to promote transparency and fairness, but it also means agents need to be meticulous in their record-keeping.

For instance, if a tenant challenges a rent increase, agents must be able to demonstrate how the new rent aligns with comparable properties in the area. This could involve gathering data on local rental trends, analysing similar listings, and maintaining detailed records of their findings. Failure to comply could result in disputes being escalated to the First-tier Tribunal, where agents will need to present robust evidence to support their case.

Practical Guidance for Agents

To assist letting agents in navigating these changes, Rightmove recently hosted a webinar featuring industry consultant David d’Orton-Gibson. The session provided practical advice on several key areas, including how to handle tenant requests to change rent payment dates and how to prepare for potential tribunal challenges. The webinar emphasised the importance of consistency and clarity in rent-setting procedures, helping agents to approach their responsibilities with confidence.

Jason Charles, head of education at Rightmove, highlighted the need for agents to stay informed and adaptable. He stated, “The Renters’ Rights Act has fundamentally altered how rents are managed, and agents must feel equipped to meet these new expectations. Our webinar was designed to provide actionable insights, ensuring agents can operate with clarity and consistency in this evolving landscape.”

Preparing for the Future

As the industry continues to adapt to the Renters’ Rights Act, many letting agents are reviewing their internal processes to ensure compliance. This includes updating procedures for documenting market rents, training staff on the new requirements, and implementing systems to handle tenant requests efficiently. By taking proactive steps now, agents can minimise the risk of disputes and ensure smoother operations moving forward.

For those seeking further guidance, resources such as webinars and industry consultations can be invaluable. Staying informed and seeking expert advice could help agents navigate the complexities of the new legislation whilst maintaining positive relationships with tenants.

The Renters’ Rights Act represents a significant shift in the private rented sector, placing greater emphasis on transparency and fairness. Letting agents who take the time to understand and implement the new requirements will be better positioned to succeed in this changing landscape.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The Renters’ Rights Act has introduced significant changes to how rents are set and managed in the private rented sector, leaving letting agents with new responsibilities and challenges. With the legislation now in effect, agents are required to adapt their processes to ensure compliance, particularly when it comes to evidencing market rents and handling tenant requests. To help navigate these changes, industry experts are offering guidance, including a recent webinar hosted by Rightmove.

Understanding the New Rent-Setting Requirements

One of the key aspects of the Renters’ Rights Act is the requirement for letting agents to provide clear evidence when setting or reviewing rents. This means agents must now document their decisions thoroughly, ensuring they can justify rental amounts based on market conditions. The legislation aims to promote transparency and fairness, but it also means agents need to be meticulous in their record-keeping.

For instance, if a tenant challenges a rent increase, agents must be able to demonstrate how the new rent aligns with comparable properties in the area. This could involve gathering data on local rental trends, analysing similar listings, and maintaining detailed records of their findings. Failure to comply could result in disputes being escalated to the First-tier Tribunal, where agents will need to present robust evidence to support their case.

Practical Guidance for Agents

To assist letting agents in navigating these changes, Rightmove recently hosted a webinar featuring industry consultant David d’Orton-Gibson. The session provided practical advice on several key areas, including how to handle tenant requests to change rent payment dates and how to prepare for potential tribunal challenges. The webinar emphasised the importance of consistency and clarity in rent-setting procedures, helping agents to approach their responsibilities with confidence.

Jason Charles, head of education at Rightmove, highlighted the need for agents to stay informed and adaptable. He stated, “The Renters’ Rights Act has fundamentally altered how rents are managed, and agents must feel equipped to meet these new expectations. Our webinar was designed to provide actionable insights, ensuring agents can operate with clarity and consistency in this evolving landscape.”

Preparing for the Future

As the industry continues to adapt to the Renters’ Rights Act, many letting agents are reviewing their internal processes to ensure compliance. This includes updating procedures for documenting market rents, training staff on the new requirements, and implementing systems to handle tenant requests efficiently. By taking proactive steps now, agents can minimise the risk of disputes and ensure smoother operations moving forward.

For those seeking further guidance, resources such as webinars and industry consultations can be invaluable. Staying informed and seeking expert advice could help agents navigate the complexities of the new legislation whilst maintaining positive relationships with tenants.

The Renters’ Rights Act represents a significant shift in the private rented sector, placing greater emphasis on transparency and fairness. Letting agents who take the time to understand and implement the new requirements will be better positioned to succeed in this changing landscape.

item.title: New tool aims to cut weeks off property transactions
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Property technology firm iamproperty has launched a new service designed to accelerate the homebuying process by preparing essential documentation earlier. The "Sale Ready" initiative aims to streamline Private Treaty transactions by collating critical property information, legal paperwork and searches at the listing stage, potentially reducing completion times by up to four weeks.

Modernising the traditional sales process

The launch coincides with government proposals to reform England's property purchasing system through increased upfront information requirements. Industry experts have long highlighted how delayed documentation contributes to lengthy transaction times and collapsed sales.

Sale Ready incorporates several innovative features:

  • AI-powered analysis of title registers and local authority search timelines
  • Early preparation of material information packs
  • Integration with conveyancing services from instruction

iamproperty reports having processed nearly 20,000 upfront information packs for auction transactions in 2023 alone, demonstrating the potential for similar approaches in Private Treaty sales.

Addressing industry challenges

Ben Ridgway, iamproperty Co-Founder, commented: "The auction sector has shown how streamlined transactions can operate efficiently. Sale Ready applies these principles to traditional sales, helping close the expectation gap between consumers' desires for speed and the current reality."

The service specifically targets three common pain points:

  • Delays caused by late-arriving documentation
  • Uncertainty about property information
  • Inconsistent processes between different transactions

Ridgway added: "This isn't just about technology - it's about changing how the industry operates. When sellers begin the process fully prepared, everyone benefits from greater transparency and efficiency."

As the property market continues evolving, solutions like Sale Ready may help bridge the gap between current practices and the government's proposed reforms. For estate agents, early adoption of such systems could provide competitive advantages in an increasingly demanding marketplace.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Property technology firm iamproperty has launched a new service designed to accelerate the homebuying process by preparing essential documentation earlier. The "Sale Ready" initiative aims to streamline Private Treaty transactions by collating critical property information, legal paperwork and searches at the listing stage, potentially reducing completion times by up to four weeks.

Modernising the traditional sales process

The launch coincides with government proposals to reform England's property purchasing system through increased upfront information requirements. Industry experts have long highlighted how delayed documentation contributes to lengthy transaction times and collapsed sales.

Sale Ready incorporates several innovative features:

  • AI-powered analysis of title registers and local authority search timelines
  • Early preparation of material information packs
  • Integration with conveyancing services from instruction

iamproperty reports having processed nearly 20,000 upfront information packs for auction transactions in 2023 alone, demonstrating the potential for similar approaches in Private Treaty sales.

Addressing industry challenges

Ben Ridgway, iamproperty Co-Founder, commented: "The auction sector has shown how streamlined transactions can operate efficiently. Sale Ready applies these principles to traditional sales, helping close the expectation gap between consumers' desires for speed and the current reality."

The service specifically targets three common pain points:

  • Delays caused by late-arriving documentation
  • Uncertainty about property information
  • Inconsistent processes between different transactions

Ridgway added: "This isn't just about technology - it's about changing how the industry operates. When sellers begin the process fully prepared, everyone benefits from greater transparency and efficiency."

As the property market continues evolving, solutions like Sale Ready may help bridge the gap between current practices and the government's proposed reforms. For estate agents, early adoption of such systems could provide competitive advantages in an increasingly demanding marketplace.

item.title: A decade on from Brexit: Has the UK housing market paid the price?
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

A decade after the UK's Brexit referendum, opinions remain sharply divided on its long-term impact on the housing market. Whilst some industry experts argue that global factors like the pandemic and rising interest rates have played a greater role, others maintain that leaving the EU has dampened economic growth and housing demand. New research suggests many homeowners share concerns about Brexit's lasting effects on property prices and mortgage affordability.

Public perception of Brexit's housing impact

Recent findings from specialist lender Together reveal that 50% of Britons believe Brexit has negatively affected the housing market, with 28% stating the impact has been significant. Only 24% view Brexit as having a positive influence, with just 9% describing the improvement as substantial. These views emerge amidst ongoing challenges including higher mortgage rates and stretched affordability, which may be colouring public sentiment.

The research highlights notable regional variations in attitudes. Scottish respondents were most likely to perceive substantial Brexit-related harm to housing (47%), followed by residents in the North East (37%) and North West (33%). Londoners showed particularly low enthusiasm, with only 19% believing Brexit helped the property market, falling to 11% in the West Midlands.

Weighing Brexit against other economic factors

Mortgage experts caution against attributing all housing market challenges solely to Brexit. The past decade has seen unprecedented global events including the COVID-19 pandemic, inflationary pressures and rapid Bank of England base rate increases from historic lows. These factors have arguably had more immediate and measurable impacts on mortgage affordability and buyer confidence.

Scott Clay, director at Together, noted: "Whilst Brexit undoubtedly introduced economic uncertainty, separating its effects from other major events proves challenging. Many households have faced growing affordability pressures throughout this period, regardless of their views on EU membership."

For those considering their mortgage options, the current landscape may warrant careful review of available deals. Borrowers approaching the end of fixed-rate terms might particularly benefit from exploring the full range of products, as lenders continue to adjust offerings in response to changing economic conditions.

As the UK property market continues to evolve, the full legacy of Brexit may take further time to assess. What remains clear is that homeowners and prospective buyers face a complex set of factors influencing their housing decisions, with personal circumstances increasingly determining the best way forward.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

A decade after the UK's Brexit referendum, opinions remain sharply divided on its long-term impact on the housing market. Whilst some industry experts argue that global factors like the pandemic and rising interest rates have played a greater role, others maintain that leaving the EU has dampened economic growth and housing demand. New research suggests many homeowners share concerns about Brexit's lasting effects on property prices and mortgage affordability.

Public perception of Brexit's housing impact

Recent findings from specialist lender Together reveal that 50% of Britons believe Brexit has negatively affected the housing market, with 28% stating the impact has been significant. Only 24% view Brexit as having a positive influence, with just 9% describing the improvement as substantial. These views emerge amidst ongoing challenges including higher mortgage rates and stretched affordability, which may be colouring public sentiment.

The research highlights notable regional variations in attitudes. Scottish respondents were most likely to perceive substantial Brexit-related harm to housing (47%), followed by residents in the North East (37%) and North West (33%). Londoners showed particularly low enthusiasm, with only 19% believing Brexit helped the property market, falling to 11% in the West Midlands.

Weighing Brexit against other economic factors

Mortgage experts caution against attributing all housing market challenges solely to Brexit. The past decade has seen unprecedented global events including the COVID-19 pandemic, inflationary pressures and rapid Bank of England base rate increases from historic lows. These factors have arguably had more immediate and measurable impacts on mortgage affordability and buyer confidence.

Scott Clay, director at Together, noted: "Whilst Brexit undoubtedly introduced economic uncertainty, separating its effects from other major events proves challenging. Many households have faced growing affordability pressures throughout this period, regardless of their views on EU membership."

For those considering their mortgage options, the current landscape may warrant careful review of available deals. Borrowers approaching the end of fixed-rate terms might particularly benefit from exploring the full range of products, as lenders continue to adjust offerings in response to changing economic conditions.

As the UK property market continues to evolve, the full legacy of Brexit may take further time to assess. What remains clear is that homeowners and prospective buyers face a complex set of factors influencing their housing decisions, with personal circumstances increasingly determining the best way forward.

item.title: Tenant Phishing Scams and the UK Letting Industry’s Email Problem
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK rental market has seen a significant shift towards digital processes in recent years, with email becoming the primary channel for communications between landlords, letting agents and tenants. Whilst this has streamlined many aspects of property management, from tenancy agreements to maintenance requests, it has also opened the door to a rising wave of sophisticated phishing scams targeting all parties in the lettings chain.

The Growing Threat of Rental Scams

Cybercriminals are increasingly exploiting the digital nature of modern lettings to launch phishing attacks. Common tactics include:

  • Fake deposit requests appearing to come from legitimate letting agents
  • Spoofed landlord emails requesting urgent rent payments to new accounts
  • Compromised agent accounts sending malicious links regarding tenancy renewals

These scams often appear highly convincing, using stolen logos, closely mimicking genuine email addresses, and creating a false sense of urgency to pressure victims into acting without proper verification.

Protecting Against Rental Phishing Attempts

Both landlords and tenants might consider several precautions to reduce their vulnerability to these scams:

  • Verifying any payment request by calling the sender on a known, trusted number
  • Checking email addresses carefully for subtle misspellings or unusual domains
  • Being wary of messages demanding immediate action or containing suspicious links
  • Using secure portals for sensitive communications where possible

Letting agents could also review their cybersecurity measures, including staff training on identifying phishing attempts and implementing two-factor authentication for email accounts.

As the rental market continues to embrace digital solutions, awareness of these threats remains crucial. Both property professionals and tenants may benefit from staying informed about common scam tactics and maintaining healthy scepticism when receiving unexpected financial requests via email.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK rental market has seen a significant shift towards digital processes in recent years, with email becoming the primary channel for communications between landlords, letting agents and tenants. Whilst this has streamlined many aspects of property management, from tenancy agreements to maintenance requests, it has also opened the door to a rising wave of sophisticated phishing scams targeting all parties in the lettings chain.

The Growing Threat of Rental Scams

Cybercriminals are increasingly exploiting the digital nature of modern lettings to launch phishing attacks. Common tactics include:

  • Fake deposit requests appearing to come from legitimate letting agents
  • Spoofed landlord emails requesting urgent rent payments to new accounts
  • Compromised agent accounts sending malicious links regarding tenancy renewals

These scams often appear highly convincing, using stolen logos, closely mimicking genuine email addresses, and creating a false sense of urgency to pressure victims into acting without proper verification.

Protecting Against Rental Phishing Attempts

Both landlords and tenants might consider several precautions to reduce their vulnerability to these scams:

  • Verifying any payment request by calling the sender on a known, trusted number
  • Checking email addresses carefully for subtle misspellings or unusual domains
  • Being wary of messages demanding immediate action or containing suspicious links
  • Using secure portals for sensitive communications where possible

Letting agents could also review their cybersecurity measures, including staff training on identifying phishing attempts and implementing two-factor authentication for email accounts.

As the rental market continues to embrace digital solutions, awareness of these threats remains crucial. Both property professionals and tenants may benefit from staying informed about common scam tactics and maintaining healthy scepticism when receiving unexpected financial requests via email.

item.title: Landlord fines hit up to £7,000 – but will councils collect them?
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

New government measures allowing councils to impose fines of up to £7,000 on landlords failing to maintain rental properties have drawn mixed reactions from industry experts. Whilst the steeper penalties aim to improve housing standards, questions remain about whether local authorities possess the resources to enforce them effectively.

Landlords face tougher penalties for poor property conditions

The updated Housing, Health and Safety Rating System (HHSRS) introduces substantially higher fines for landlords who neglect serious hazards in their rental properties. The National Residential Landlords Association (NRLA) has welcomed the principle of stronger deterrents but highlights concerns about inconsistent enforcement across local councils.

NRLA chief executive Ben Beadle noted: "Responsible landlords who maintain their properties properly have nothing to fear from these changes. The real challenge lies in tackling the minority of rogue operators who give the sector a bad name." The association has worked with government to develop clearer guidance for both landlords and tenants on identifying and resolving property hazards.

Enforcement concerns overshadow penalty increases

Industry analysis suggests current enforcement measures may be falling short. NRLA Freedom of Information requests reveal that between 2023 and 2025, councils collected just 25% of fines issued to private landlords. This low recovery rate raises questions about whether increased penalty levels alone can drive meaningful improvements.

Beadle argues that without proper resourcing for enforcement teams, higher fines may prove ineffective: "The priority should be ensuring councils have adequate staffing and funding to pursue rogue landlords consistently. Simply increasing potential fines misses the point if authorities lack capacity to enforce them."

The NRLA recommends annual reporting requirements for councils to improve transparency around enforcement activity. They also suggest a national database of landlord convictions and banning orders to help authorities target repeat offenders more effectively.

As the new penalty regime takes effect, its success may depend less on the size of fines and more on whether local authorities receive the necessary support to apply them consistently. Both tenants and responsible landlords will be watching closely to see if these measures deliver tangible improvements to rental property standards.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

New government measures allowing councils to impose fines of up to £7,000 on landlords failing to maintain rental properties have drawn mixed reactions from industry experts. Whilst the steeper penalties aim to improve housing standards, questions remain about whether local authorities possess the resources to enforce them effectively.

Landlords face tougher penalties for poor property conditions

The updated Housing, Health and Safety Rating System (HHSRS) introduces substantially higher fines for landlords who neglect serious hazards in their rental properties. The National Residential Landlords Association (NRLA) has welcomed the principle of stronger deterrents but highlights concerns about inconsistent enforcement across local councils.

NRLA chief executive Ben Beadle noted: "Responsible landlords who maintain their properties properly have nothing to fear from these changes. The real challenge lies in tackling the minority of rogue operators who give the sector a bad name." The association has worked with government to develop clearer guidance for both landlords and tenants on identifying and resolving property hazards.

Enforcement concerns overshadow penalty increases

Industry analysis suggests current enforcement measures may be falling short. NRLA Freedom of Information requests reveal that between 2023 and 2025, councils collected just 25% of fines issued to private landlords. This low recovery rate raises questions about whether increased penalty levels alone can drive meaningful improvements.

Beadle argues that without proper resourcing for enforcement teams, higher fines may prove ineffective: "The priority should be ensuring councils have adequate staffing and funding to pursue rogue landlords consistently. Simply increasing potential fines misses the point if authorities lack capacity to enforce them."

The NRLA recommends annual reporting requirements for councils to improve transparency around enforcement activity. They also suggest a national database of landlord convictions and banning orders to help authorities target repeat offenders more effectively.

As the new penalty regime takes effect, its success may depend less on the size of fines and more on whether local authorities receive the necessary support to apply them consistently. Both tenants and responsible landlords will be watching closely to see if these measures deliver tangible improvements to rental property standards.

item.title: Can the government still hit its housing target?
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

England’s housing market appears unlikely to meet the government’s annual target of 300,000 new homes, with recent forecasts suggesting a significant shortfall over the coming years. According to property consultancy Savills, completions are expected to average just 167,500 per year until 2029/30, leaving the country well below its housing goals. The decline follows a 4.1% drop in new home completions in the year to March 2025, with further reductions anticipated in the next two years.

Challenges in housing delivery

Several factors are contributing to the slowdown in housebuilding. Planning consents have fallen by 39% over the past three years, while housing starts have dropped by 31%. Build costs have risen sharply—up 17.5% since early 2022—while house price growth has lagged at just 4.5%, squeezing developer margins. Affordability pressures and higher mortgage rates have also dampened demand, making many projects less viable.

Savills predicts completions could fall to around 152,000 in 2026/27, far below the levels needed to address the housing shortage. The consultancy notes that while planning reforms may eventually help, it could take at least 18 months before any positive impact is seen in completion figures.

Glimmers of hope in planning reforms

Despite the challenging outlook, there are some signs of improvement at the early stages of the planning process. Residential planning applications have increased by 44% in the past year, and appeal success rates have improved. However, translating these early-stage gains into actual housing delivery will take time.

Emily Williams, director of residential research at Savills, commented: "England’s housing delivery has shown resilience, but the underlying challenges are growing. With fewer consents and starts, the pipeline is shrinking, while affordability and cost pressures limit viability. While planning reforms are a step in the right direction, boosting demand may be the quickest way to improve delivery in the short term."

For now, the gap between housing targets and actual supply looks set to persist, leaving policymakers and industry stakeholders searching for solutions to accelerate construction and improve affordability.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

England’s housing market appears unlikely to meet the government’s annual target of 300,000 new homes, with recent forecasts suggesting a significant shortfall over the coming years. According to property consultancy Savills, completions are expected to average just 167,500 per year until 2029/30, leaving the country well below its housing goals. The decline follows a 4.1% drop in new home completions in the year to March 2025, with further reductions anticipated in the next two years.

Challenges in housing delivery

Several factors are contributing to the slowdown in housebuilding. Planning consents have fallen by 39% over the past three years, while housing starts have dropped by 31%. Build costs have risen sharply—up 17.5% since early 2022—while house price growth has lagged at just 4.5%, squeezing developer margins. Affordability pressures and higher mortgage rates have also dampened demand, making many projects less viable.

Savills predicts completions could fall to around 152,000 in 2026/27, far below the levels needed to address the housing shortage. The consultancy notes that while planning reforms may eventually help, it could take at least 18 months before any positive impact is seen in completion figures.

Glimmers of hope in planning reforms

Despite the challenging outlook, there are some signs of improvement at the early stages of the planning process. Residential planning applications have increased by 44% in the past year, and appeal success rates have improved. However, translating these early-stage gains into actual housing delivery will take time.

Emily Williams, director of residential research at Savills, commented: "England’s housing delivery has shown resilience, but the underlying challenges are growing. With fewer consents and starts, the pipeline is shrinking, while affordability and cost pressures limit viability. While planning reforms are a step in the right direction, boosting demand may be the quickest way to improve delivery in the short term."

For now, the gap between housing targets and actual supply looks set to persist, leaving policymakers and industry stakeholders searching for solutions to accelerate construction and improve affordability.

item.title: Price over postcode: Estate agents face a new Gen Z buyer mindset
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Gen Z Buyers Prioritise Affordability Over Location

Younger homebuyers are increasingly willing to compromise on their ideal location to secure a more affordable property, according to recent research. A Barclays report reveals that one in five Gen Z purchasers would consider moving over 25 miles from their preferred area to get onto the housing ladder. With a quarter admitting they cannot afford to buy where they'd ideally live, price has overtaken postcode as the primary concern for this demographic.

Whilst average deposit requirements have fallen by 16.4% year-on-year to £57,209, saving for a deposit remains the biggest hurdle for 36% of renters. The data suggests estate agents may need to adapt their approach as buyer priorities shift, with affordability now driving decisions more than traditional location preferences amongst first-time buyers.

Market Challenges and Regional Variations

The report highlights ongoing frustrations with the home-moving process, with nearly 90% of buyers and sellers experiencing delays. Conveyancing issues emerged as the most common cause, followed by estate agency complications and limited property availability. The average time between mortgage offer and completion has increased by 21.7% compared to last year.

Regional deposit variations show significant changes:

  • Greater London saw the sharpest decline at 27.2% (£186,960 to £136,057)
  • East Anglia deposits fell 23.5% (£72,014 to £55,063)
  • Northern Ireland bucked the trend with a 14.9% increase (£35,679 to £41,008)

Despite these challenges, market confidence appears to be growing. The research indicates 16% of Gen Z renters are actively property hunting, whilst remortgaging activity has risen to 40.6% of completions - up from 30.7% last year. Homeowners might consider reviewing their current deals as refinancing becomes more attractive in the current climate.

As the market evolves, both buyers and industry professionals may need to adjust expectations. With affordability constraints reshaping priorities, flexibility could prove key to successful transactions in today's challenging environment.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Gen Z Buyers Prioritise Affordability Over Location

Younger homebuyers are increasingly willing to compromise on their ideal location to secure a more affordable property, according to recent research. A Barclays report reveals that one in five Gen Z purchasers would consider moving over 25 miles from their preferred area to get onto the housing ladder. With a quarter admitting they cannot afford to buy where they'd ideally live, price has overtaken postcode as the primary concern for this demographic.

Whilst average deposit requirements have fallen by 16.4% year-on-year to £57,209, saving for a deposit remains the biggest hurdle for 36% of renters. The data suggests estate agents may need to adapt their approach as buyer priorities shift, with affordability now driving decisions more than traditional location preferences amongst first-time buyers.

Market Challenges and Regional Variations

The report highlights ongoing frustrations with the home-moving process, with nearly 90% of buyers and sellers experiencing delays. Conveyancing issues emerged as the most common cause, followed by estate agency complications and limited property availability. The average time between mortgage offer and completion has increased by 21.7% compared to last year.

Regional deposit variations show significant changes:

  • Greater London saw the sharpest decline at 27.2% (£186,960 to £136,057)
  • East Anglia deposits fell 23.5% (£72,014 to £55,063)
  • Northern Ireland bucked the trend with a 14.9% increase (£35,679 to £41,008)

Despite these challenges, market confidence appears to be growing. The research indicates 16% of Gen Z renters are actively property hunting, whilst remortgaging activity has risen to 40.6% of completions - up from 30.7% last year. Homeowners might consider reviewing their current deals as refinancing becomes more attractive in the current climate.

As the market evolves, both buyers and industry professionals may need to adjust expectations. With affordability constraints reshaping priorities, flexibility could prove key to successful transactions in today's challenging environment.

item.title: Property firm enters administration
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Devonshire Homes, a regional housebuilder based in Devon, has entered administration following financial difficulties. Joint administrators Sarah Collins and Jonathan Marston from Alvarez & Marsal Europe have been appointed to manage the company’s affairs. The firm, chaired by former Conservative MP David Heathcoat-Amory, reported a turnover of £52m in its latest accounts but recorded pre-tax losses of £137,024 for the year ending September 2024.

Impact on Current Developments

The administration affects two ongoing projects: The Grange in Bideford, a 225-home development, and St Michael’s Reach in Penzance, which was set to deliver 320 properties. However, administrators have confirmed that other Devonshire-branded developments and associated companies remain unaffected. Homebuyers with reservations on these sites may wish to contact the administrators for clarity on project completion timelines.

In its most recent financial statements, the company disclosed a £1.3m anticipated loss linked to a partly completed development featuring bespoke timber-frame homes. Devonshire Homes admitted this project fell outside its core business model, with construction costs significantly exceeding initial estimates. The firm subsequently set aside £1.12m as a provision against the expected total loss.

Financial Challenges and Auditor Warnings

Founded in the 1990s, Devonshire Homes employed around 80 staff during the reporting period but faced mounting financial pressures. Last October, auditors highlighted a "material uncertainty related to going concern" in the company’s accounts, flagging the need for additional funding to sustain operations. The firm had already signalled a shift in strategy, stating future land acquisitions would focus solely on schemes using its standard house types to mitigate risk.

For prospective buyers concerned about purchasing from developers in financial distress, it could be worth checking a company’s latest filed accounts and any insolvency notices before committing. Those affected by the administration might consider seeking independent legal advice regarding their contractual position.

While the housing market continues to face challenges, this case underscores the importance of thorough due diligence for both developers and purchasers in the current economic climate.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Devonshire Homes, a regional housebuilder based in Devon, has entered administration following financial difficulties. Joint administrators Sarah Collins and Jonathan Marston from Alvarez & Marsal Europe have been appointed to manage the company’s affairs. The firm, chaired by former Conservative MP David Heathcoat-Amory, reported a turnover of £52m in its latest accounts but recorded pre-tax losses of £137,024 for the year ending September 2024.

Impact on Current Developments

The administration affects two ongoing projects: The Grange in Bideford, a 225-home development, and St Michael’s Reach in Penzance, which was set to deliver 320 properties. However, administrators have confirmed that other Devonshire-branded developments and associated companies remain unaffected. Homebuyers with reservations on these sites may wish to contact the administrators for clarity on project completion timelines.

In its most recent financial statements, the company disclosed a £1.3m anticipated loss linked to a partly completed development featuring bespoke timber-frame homes. Devonshire Homes admitted this project fell outside its core business model, with construction costs significantly exceeding initial estimates. The firm subsequently set aside £1.12m as a provision against the expected total loss.

Financial Challenges and Auditor Warnings

Founded in the 1990s, Devonshire Homes employed around 80 staff during the reporting period but faced mounting financial pressures. Last October, auditors highlighted a "material uncertainty related to going concern" in the company’s accounts, flagging the need for additional funding to sustain operations. The firm had already signalled a shift in strategy, stating future land acquisitions would focus solely on schemes using its standard house types to mitigate risk.

For prospective buyers concerned about purchasing from developers in financial distress, it could be worth checking a company’s latest filed accounts and any insolvency notices before committing. Those affected by the administration might consider seeking independent legal advice regarding their contractual position.

While the housing market continues to face challenges, this case underscores the importance of thorough due diligence for both developers and purchasers in the current economic climate.

item.title: Landlords face £7,000 fines from today under new rental rules
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

From today, landlords in England could face fines of up to £7,000 if they fail to address serious hazards in their rental properties. These new enforcement powers, introduced under the Renters’ Rights Act, empower local authorities to issue financial penalties for unresolved issues such as damp, mould, fire risks, electrical faults, and structural defects. The measures aim to strengthen councils’ ability to ensure housing standards in the private rented sector.

What Do the New Rules Mean for Landlords?

The updated regulations grant local councils greater authority to take swift action against landlords who neglect their responsibilities. Alongside fines, councils can compel landlords to carry out necessary repairs, undertake emergency remedial works, and recover costs if landlords fail to act. Housing Secretary Steve Reed has urged local authorities to utilise these powers fully, emphasising that tenants deserve safe and secure homes.

For landlords, this means ensuring their properties meet health and safety standards. Issues like severe damp or mould, which were previously overlooked by some, could now result in significant financial penalties. The government hopes these measures will deter rogue landlords and improve living conditions for renters.

What Should Tenants Know?

Tenants are being encouraged to report poor housing conditions without fear of retaliation. The abolition of Section 21 "no-fault" evictions under the Renters’ Rights Act provides greater protection for renters, making it easier to raise concerns about unsafe or substandard living conditions. Ministers believe this will empower tenants to hold landlords accountable.

Additionally, the Housing Health and Safety Rating System (HHSRS) in England is being updated to help councils identify hazards more effectively. This reform is expected to streamline enforcement processes, ensuring quicker action is taken when tenants are living in unsafe environments.

Will the Measures Be Effective?

While the introduction of £7,000 fines has been welcomed by housing campaigners, questions remain about how effectively councils will enforce the new rules. Ben Twomey of Generation Rent described the fines as an "essential step" towards improving rental property standards. However, some critics have raised concerns about whether local authorities have the resources to implement these measures consistently.

Clara Collingwood of the Renters’ Reform Coalition highlighted the importance of tenants exercising their new rights. With stronger protections in place, renters may feel more confident in demanding better living conditions. However, the success of these measures will ultimately depend on proactive enforcement by councils and landlords’ willingness to comply.

These changes mark a significant shift in the rental landscape, aiming to create safer and more secure housing for tenants whilst holding landlords accountable for maintaining their properties.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

From today, landlords in England could face fines of up to £7,000 if they fail to address serious hazards in their rental properties. These new enforcement powers, introduced under the Renters’ Rights Act, empower local authorities to issue financial penalties for unresolved issues such as damp, mould, fire risks, electrical faults, and structural defects. The measures aim to strengthen councils’ ability to ensure housing standards in the private rented sector.

What Do the New Rules Mean for Landlords?

The updated regulations grant local councils greater authority to take swift action against landlords who neglect their responsibilities. Alongside fines, councils can compel landlords to carry out necessary repairs, undertake emergency remedial works, and recover costs if landlords fail to act. Housing Secretary Steve Reed has urged local authorities to utilise these powers fully, emphasising that tenants deserve safe and secure homes.

For landlords, this means ensuring their properties meet health and safety standards. Issues like severe damp or mould, which were previously overlooked by some, could now result in significant financial penalties. The government hopes these measures will deter rogue landlords and improve living conditions for renters.

What Should Tenants Know?

Tenants are being encouraged to report poor housing conditions without fear of retaliation. The abolition of Section 21 "no-fault" evictions under the Renters’ Rights Act provides greater protection for renters, making it easier to raise concerns about unsafe or substandard living conditions. Ministers believe this will empower tenants to hold landlords accountable.

Additionally, the Housing Health and Safety Rating System (HHSRS) in England is being updated to help councils identify hazards more effectively. This reform is expected to streamline enforcement processes, ensuring quicker action is taken when tenants are living in unsafe environments.

Will the Measures Be Effective?

While the introduction of £7,000 fines has been welcomed by housing campaigners, questions remain about how effectively councils will enforce the new rules. Ben Twomey of Generation Rent described the fines as an "essential step" towards improving rental property standards. However, some critics have raised concerns about whether local authorities have the resources to implement these measures consistently.

Clara Collingwood of the Renters’ Reform Coalition highlighted the importance of tenants exercising their new rights. With stronger protections in place, renters may feel more confident in demanding better living conditions. However, the success of these measures will ultimately depend on proactive enforcement by councils and landlords’ willingness to comply.

These changes mark a significant shift in the rental landscape, aiming to create safer and more secure housing for tenants whilst holding landlords accountable for maintaining their properties.

item.title: Why an Andy Burnham premiership could accelerate regional growth
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The potential for Andy Burnham to become Prime Minister has sparked discussions about how his leadership could influence regional growth, particularly in areas like Greater Manchester. With extensive experience as a city-region mayor, Burnham has demonstrated how devolved powers can stimulate local economies when combined with strategic investment and private sector collaboration.

Greater Manchester's devolution success story

Greater Manchester has become a blueprint for regional development under Burnham's leadership. The area has shown how cross-party cooperation and long-term planning can foster thriving sectors including life sciences, digital innovation and advanced manufacturing. This collaborative approach has also supported the expansion of universities and town centre regeneration projects.

The forthcoming English Devolution and Community Empowerment Act 2026 aims to strengthen mayoral powers further. However, experts suggest that additional funding and flexibility from central government will be crucial to translate these powers into tangible improvements in housing, transport and infrastructure.

Housing and infrastructure challenges

Current housing policies often fail to address regional variations in demand and affordability. A more localised approach could enable authorities to develop tailored solutions, potentially including:

  • Increased public-sector housing projects
  • Innovative partnerships with private developers
  • Expansion of Build to Rent schemes

Infrastructure development remains equally critical, with transport networks requiring particular attention in the North West. Progress on projects like Northern Powerhouse Rail could transform economic connectivity between Manchester, Liverpool and surrounding regions. Improved transport links typically lead to stronger labour markets, more efficient supply chains and greater business investment opportunities.

As the debate about regional growth continues, many will be watching how Burnham's potential premiership might accelerate these developments. The success of Greater Manchester's model suggests that combining local decision-making with adequate resources could benefit other regions across the UK.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The potential for Andy Burnham to become Prime Minister has sparked discussions about how his leadership could influence regional growth, particularly in areas like Greater Manchester. With extensive experience as a city-region mayor, Burnham has demonstrated how devolved powers can stimulate local economies when combined with strategic investment and private sector collaboration.

Greater Manchester's devolution success story

Greater Manchester has become a blueprint for regional development under Burnham's leadership. The area has shown how cross-party cooperation and long-term planning can foster thriving sectors including life sciences, digital innovation and advanced manufacturing. This collaborative approach has also supported the expansion of universities and town centre regeneration projects.

The forthcoming English Devolution and Community Empowerment Act 2026 aims to strengthen mayoral powers further. However, experts suggest that additional funding and flexibility from central government will be crucial to translate these powers into tangible improvements in housing, transport and infrastructure.

Housing and infrastructure challenges

Current housing policies often fail to address regional variations in demand and affordability. A more localised approach could enable authorities to develop tailored solutions, potentially including:

  • Increased public-sector housing projects
  • Innovative partnerships with private developers
  • Expansion of Build to Rent schemes

Infrastructure development remains equally critical, with transport networks requiring particular attention in the North West. Progress on projects like Northern Powerhouse Rail could transform economic connectivity between Manchester, Liverpool and surrounding regions. Improved transport links typically lead to stronger labour markets, more efficient supply chains and greater business investment opportunities.

As the debate about regional growth continues, many will be watching how Burnham's potential premiership might accelerate these developments. The success of Greater Manchester's model suggests that combining local decision-making with adequate resources could benefit other regions across the UK.

item.title: Renters’ Rights Act has so far failed to rattle the lettings market
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK lettings market has shown unexpected resilience in the month following the implementation of the Renters’ Rights Act, with recent data suggesting stable conditions for both tenants and landlords. Contrary to some predictions, the legislation appears to have caused minimal disruption, with rising demand, improved property availability and steady affordability levels characterising May’s rental landscape.

Market activity shows seasonal strength

Industry figures reveal a 13.7% monthly increase in tenant demand as the market entered the traditional summer peak period. Whilst activity remains slightly below last year’s levels, the upward trend suggests a continuing recovery in the rental sector. Simultaneously, competition for properties has eased, with the ratio of registered renters to new listings falling by 8.6% annually and 5.5% monthly.

Property supply has shown encouraging growth, with new listings rising by 3% compared to May 2023 and 5.7% month-on-month. This expansion in available stock has provided tenants with more options whilst helping to maintain balance in the market. The stability in new instructions also indicates that landlord confidence has held firm despite the recent legislative changes.

Affordability remains steady

Rental affordability has shown little sign of significant pressure, with average tenant budgets increasing by just 0.3% year-on-year to £548 per week. The modest 2.1% monthly rise aligns with typical seasonal patterns rather than indicating any fundamental market shift. These figures suggest the Renters’ Rights Act has not immediately translated into higher costs for tenants, as some had feared.

Regional variations tell an interesting story, with the West London market seeing particularly strong growth in new instructions (up 36% annually) alongside a 17% increase in renter registrations. In contrast, Central London experienced declines in both supply and demand, highlighting the importance of local market knowledge for both landlords and tenants.

Industry experts note that the build-to-rent sector continues to expand, with London’s pipeline growing by nearly 15% over the past year. For landlords and property operators, the current climate emphasises the importance of clear marketing and working with agents who understand local dynamics. As the summer lettings season progresses, the market appears well-positioned to accommodate both tenant demand and landlord requirements without significant disruption.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK lettings market has shown unexpected resilience in the month following the implementation of the Renters’ Rights Act, with recent data suggesting stable conditions for both tenants and landlords. Contrary to some predictions, the legislation appears to have caused minimal disruption, with rising demand, improved property availability and steady affordability levels characterising May’s rental landscape.

Market activity shows seasonal strength

Industry figures reveal a 13.7% monthly increase in tenant demand as the market entered the traditional summer peak period. Whilst activity remains slightly below last year’s levels, the upward trend suggests a continuing recovery in the rental sector. Simultaneously, competition for properties has eased, with the ratio of registered renters to new listings falling by 8.6% annually and 5.5% monthly.

Property supply has shown encouraging growth, with new listings rising by 3% compared to May 2023 and 5.7% month-on-month. This expansion in available stock has provided tenants with more options whilst helping to maintain balance in the market. The stability in new instructions also indicates that landlord confidence has held firm despite the recent legislative changes.

Affordability remains steady

Rental affordability has shown little sign of significant pressure, with average tenant budgets increasing by just 0.3% year-on-year to £548 per week. The modest 2.1% monthly rise aligns with typical seasonal patterns rather than indicating any fundamental market shift. These figures suggest the Renters’ Rights Act has not immediately translated into higher costs for tenants, as some had feared.

Regional variations tell an interesting story, with the West London market seeing particularly strong growth in new instructions (up 36% annually) alongside a 17% increase in renter registrations. In contrast, Central London experienced declines in both supply and demand, highlighting the importance of local market knowledge for both landlords and tenants.

Industry experts note that the build-to-rent sector continues to expand, with London’s pipeline growing by nearly 15% over the past year. For landlords and property operators, the current climate emphasises the importance of clear marketing and working with agents who understand local dynamics. As the summer lettings season progresses, the market appears well-positioned to accommodate both tenant demand and landlord requirements without significant disruption.

item.title: Timescale too long for homebuying reforms
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Mortgage industry professionals have expressed concerns over the proposed timeline for implementing homebuying reforms, with many suggesting the current schedule could delay crucial improvements until the next parliamentary term.

Industry Frustration Over Reform Delays

Property and finance experts have voiced disappointment at the protracted rollout of planned changes to the homebuying process. The reforms, designed to streamline property transactions and reduce fall-through rates, now face potential delays that could push full implementation beyond the current government's term.

Key stakeholders argue that the housing market requires more urgent action, particularly given current challenges around transaction times and affordability. Some industry leaders have suggested that a phased approach might deliver benefits sooner whilst maintaining the integrity of the proposed changes.

Potential Impact on Borrowers

For prospective homeowners, these delays could mean:

  • Continued lengthy transaction periods when purchasing property
  • Missed opportunities to benefit from proposed consumer protections
  • Potential uncertainty in the market during the transition period

Mortgage applicants might find it worthwhile to stay informed about these developments, as the reforms could eventually introduce significant changes to the homebuying journey. Those considering property purchases in the near future may wish to consult with mortgage advisers about how the current system operates and what improvements might lie ahead.

As the debate continues, industry observers will be watching closely to see whether the government adjusts its timetable in response to these concerns. In the meantime, borrowers and property buyers will need to navigate the existing system whilst keeping an eye on future developments.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Mortgage industry professionals have expressed concerns over the proposed timeline for implementing homebuying reforms, with many suggesting the current schedule could delay crucial improvements until the next parliamentary term.

Industry Frustration Over Reform Delays

Property and finance experts have voiced disappointment at the protracted rollout of planned changes to the homebuying process. The reforms, designed to streamline property transactions and reduce fall-through rates, now face potential delays that could push full implementation beyond the current government's term.

Key stakeholders argue that the housing market requires more urgent action, particularly given current challenges around transaction times and affordability. Some industry leaders have suggested that a phased approach might deliver benefits sooner whilst maintaining the integrity of the proposed changes.

Potential Impact on Borrowers

For prospective homeowners, these delays could mean:

  • Continued lengthy transaction periods when purchasing property
  • Missed opportunities to benefit from proposed consumer protections
  • Potential uncertainty in the market during the transition period

Mortgage applicants might find it worthwhile to stay informed about these developments, as the reforms could eventually introduce significant changes to the homebuying journey. Those considering property purchases in the near future may wish to consult with mortgage advisers about how the current system operates and what improvements might lie ahead.

As the debate continues, industry observers will be watching closely to see whether the government adjusts its timetable in response to these concerns. In the meantime, borrowers and property buyers will need to navigate the existing system whilst keeping an eye on future developments.

item.title: Buyer confidence set to improve with latest base rate hold
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Bank of England rate hold may stabilise property market sentiment

The Bank of England's recent decision to maintain the base rate at its current level could provide welcome stability for the UK property market. Industry analysts suggest this pause in rate rises may help restore confidence among prospective buyers, landlords and property investors after a period of economic uncertainty.

What the rate hold means for mortgages

Mortgage borrowers might find some relief in the rate hold, which could signal a more predictable lending environment:

  • Existing tracker mortgage holders will see no immediate change to their repayments
  • Fixed-rate mortgage products may continue to stabilise after previous volatility
  • First-time buyers could benefit from improved affordability assessments

Whilst the rate pause doesn't guarantee lower mortgage costs, it may create more favourable conditions for those considering entering the market or remortgaging in the coming months.

Long-term market outlook

Property professionals note that sustained stability in interest rates often correlates with increased transaction activity. Homeowners may wish to monitor how lenders respond to the Bank's decision, as competitive mortgage products could emerge if the hold continues.

The current climate presents potential opportunities for both residential buyers and buy-to-let investors, though market conditions remain complex. Those exploring property transactions might consider seeking independent financial advice to navigate their options effectively.

As the property market continues to adjust to the current economic landscape, the rate hold could mark an important step towards restoring balance between supply, demand and affordability across UK housing sectors.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Bank of England rate hold may stabilise property market sentiment

The Bank of England's recent decision to maintain the base rate at its current level could provide welcome stability for the UK property market. Industry analysts suggest this pause in rate rises may help restore confidence among prospective buyers, landlords and property investors after a period of economic uncertainty.

What the rate hold means for mortgages

Mortgage borrowers might find some relief in the rate hold, which could signal a more predictable lending environment:

  • Existing tracker mortgage holders will see no immediate change to their repayments
  • Fixed-rate mortgage products may continue to stabilise after previous volatility
  • First-time buyers could benefit from improved affordability assessments

Whilst the rate pause doesn't guarantee lower mortgage costs, it may create more favourable conditions for those considering entering the market or remortgaging in the coming months.

Long-term market outlook

Property professionals note that sustained stability in interest rates often correlates with increased transaction activity. Homeowners may wish to monitor how lenders respond to the Bank's decision, as competitive mortgage products could emerge if the hold continues.

The current climate presents potential opportunities for both residential buyers and buy-to-let investors, though market conditions remain complex. Those exploring property transactions might consider seeking independent financial advice to navigate their options effectively.

As the property market continues to adjust to the current economic landscape, the rate hold could mark an important step towards restoring balance between supply, demand and affordability across UK housing sectors.

item.title: Residential construction to see resurgence in 2027
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

UK Housebuilding Sector Poised for Recovery by 2027

The UK's residential construction industry could be set for a significant revival towards the latter half of this decade, with analysts predicting stronger growth from 2027 onwards. Several key factors including potentially lower mortgage rates, government planning reforms and improving market sentiment may combine to breathe new life into the sector.

Factors Driving the Anticipated Recovery

Industry observers suggest the housing market may benefit from:

  • More favourable borrowing conditions as interest rates potentially stabilise
  • Streamlined planning processes following government reforms
  • Growing confidence among developers and buyers alike
  • Continued demand for new housing stock across many UK regions

What This Could Mean for Homebuyers and Investors

Whilst the forecast recovery remains a few years away, prospective buyers and property investors might consider:

  • Monitoring regional development plans for future growth areas
  • Reviewing mortgage options as market conditions evolve
  • Keeping abreast of planning policy changes that may affect supply

The anticipated resurgence in housebuilding activity could bring welcome relief to the UK's ongoing housing shortage, potentially creating more options for homebuyers and renters in the coming years. As always, those considering property transactions may wish to seek professional financial advice tailored to their individual circumstances.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

UK Housebuilding Sector Poised for Recovery by 2027

The UK's residential construction industry could be set for a significant revival towards the latter half of this decade, with analysts predicting stronger growth from 2027 onwards. Several key factors including potentially lower mortgage rates, government planning reforms and improving market sentiment may combine to breathe new life into the sector.

Factors Driving the Anticipated Recovery

Industry observers suggest the housing market may benefit from:

  • More favourable borrowing conditions as interest rates potentially stabilise
  • Streamlined planning processes following government reforms
  • Growing confidence among developers and buyers alike
  • Continued demand for new housing stock across many UK regions

What This Could Mean for Homebuyers and Investors

Whilst the forecast recovery remains a few years away, prospective buyers and property investors might consider:

  • Monitoring regional development plans for future growth areas
  • Reviewing mortgage options as market conditions evolve
  • Keeping abreast of planning policy changes that may affect supply

The anticipated resurgence in housebuilding activity could bring welcome relief to the UK's ongoing housing shortage, potentially creating more options for homebuyers and renters in the coming years. As always, those considering property transactions may wish to seek professional financial advice tailored to their individual circumstances.

item.title: New AI tool to help landlords with MTD
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Landlords managing short-term rental properties may soon find themselves equipped with a powerful new tool to streamline their operations. A recently launched AI-powered platform aims to assist hosts by providing event-driven demand data, enabling them to optimise pricing strategies and potentially boost their revenue.

How the AI Tool Works

The platform leverages artificial intelligence to analyse market trends and local events that could influence rental demand. By offering insights into fluctuations in occupancy rates and pricing opportunities, it allows landlords to make informed decisions about their listings.
